Typically the stickman starts at some default position. There's a red block in the middle that must be avoided.\n\nIf we assume the stickman starts at some center or default position, let's think about what sequence gets us to the goal.\n\nLooking at the goal image more carefully:\n- The gray ghost (previous position) is on the left at around x=4-5\n- The black stickman (current position) is on the right at around x=15-16\n- There's a red box in the middle\n\nThe gray ghost in the goal matches the left position seen in action C (where the gray is on the left and black ends up on the right). So the last action seems to be C (move right).\n\nBut there's a red box in the middle. If the stickman just moves right (action C), it would pass through the red box. So the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nWait - but the goal shows the stickman standing on the ground on the right side, not elevated. So maybe the stickman needs to jump (A) while moving right, or jump first and then move right while in the air?\n\nActually, let me reconsider. The gray ghost shows where the stickman was before the last action. In the goal, the ghost is on the left and the stickman is on the right. The last action appears to be C (move right).\n\nBut there's a red obstacle. To avoid it, the stickman might need to jump (A) first to get elevation, then move right (C) while elevated, clearing the red box.\n\nSo the sequence could be: First get to the left side (B), then jump (A), then move right (C). But wait, we need to think about where the stickman starts.\n\nLet me assume the stickman starts in the center or at some position. \n\nLooking at the goal: the stickman is on the right (~x=15), ghost on the left (~x=4). The goal shows exactly this configuration (ghost on left, stickman on right). So C must be the last action.\n\nTo avoid the red box in the middle while going from left to right, the stickman needs to be elevated. So action A (jump) before C makes sense.\n\nBut first the stickman needs to be on the left side. If the stickman starts in the middle or some default position, B (move left) would get it to the left.\n\nSo the sequence is: B (move to left), A (jump up), C (move right while airborne, clearing the red box).\n\nWait - does the stickman start at a default position? Looking at the actions again, there's no explicit starting position given. Let me assume a reasonable starting position.\n\nIf the stickman starts in the center/right area, then B moves it left, A jumps, C moves right over the red box. Sequence: BAC.\n\nActually, I think the simplest interpretation: the stickman needs to end up on the right side, having jumped over the red box. But if starting from center, we need B first.\n\nLet me reconsider - maybe the stickman starts at a default center position. B moves left, then A jumps up, then C moves right over the obstacle. BAC seems right.\n\nBut could it be shorter? Just AC? If the stickman starts on the left already... but we don't know the starting position.\n\nLooking at the goal image ghost position and the action images, I think the simplest sequence that achieves the goal while avoiding red is: C with a jump. So AC - jump first, then move right while in the air.\n\nBut we need to be on the left first. If default position is center, we need B to go left first.\n\nI'll go with BAC as the shortest sequence, assuming we start from a default/center position. But if we start from the right side (as might be default), we need BBC... no.\n\nActually, let me reconsider the starting position. Many puzzles start with the stickman at a neutral/center position. \n\nGiven the goal shows the ghost on the left and stickman on the right, and there's a red box between them, the sequence to avoid the red box would be:\n1. Get to left position (B - move left)\n2. Jump (A)  \n3. Move right over the red box (C)\n\nSequence: BAC",
  "sequence": "BAC"
}
"{\"reasoning\":\"Let me analyze each action:\\n\\nAction A: The stickman moves upward (jumps). The gray figure shows the initial position and the black figure shows the final position, which is higher up. The dotted arrow points upward.\\n\\nAction B: The stickman moves to the left. The gray figure shows the initial position (right side) and the black figure shows the final position (left side). The dotted arrow points left.\\n\\nAction C: The stickman moves to the right. The gray figure shows the initial position (left side) and the black figure shows the final position (right side).\\n\\nNow, the GOAL state: The stickman needs to be on the right side of the image (around x=15-16), standing on the ground, with a red box in the middle area (around x=9-13, on the ground). The gray \\\"ghost\\\" showing previous position is on the left side (around x=4-5).\\n\\nThe instruction says to \\\"avoid red\\\" - meaning the stickman must not pass through the red box.\\n\\nLet me think about the starting position. Typically the stickman starts at some default position. Looking at the actions:\\n\\n- In Action B, the stickman moves from right (~x=15) to left (~x=4), roughly 11 cm left\\n- In Action C, the stickman moves from left (~x=4) to right (~x=15), roughly 11 cm right\\n- In Action A, the stickman jumps up\\n\\nThe goal shows the stickman on the right side with a gray ghost on the left. There's a red block in the middle that must be avoided.\\n\\nIf we assume the stickman starts at some center or default position, let's think about what sequence gets us to the goal.\\n\\nLooking at the goal image more carefully:\\n- The gray ghost (previous position) is on the left at around x=4-5\\n- The black stickman (current position) is on the right at around x=15-16\\n- There's a red box in the middle\\n\\nThe gray ghost in the goal matches the left position seen in action C (where the gray is on the left and black ends up on the right). So the last action seems to be C (move right).\\n\\nBut there's a red box in the middle. If the stickman just moves right (action C), it would pass through the red box. So the stickman needs to jump over it.\\n\\nWait - but the goal shows the stickman standing on the ground on the right side, not elevated. So maybe the stickman needs to jump (A) while moving right, or jump first and then move right while in the air?\\n\\nActually, let me reconsider. The gray ghost shows where the stickman was before the last action. In the goal, the ghost is on the left and the stickman is on the right. The last action appears to be C (move right).\\n\\nBut there's a red obstacle. To avoid it, the stickman might need to jump (A) first to get elevation, then move right (C) while elevated, clearing the red box.\\n\\nSo the sequence could be: First get to the left side (B), then jump (A), then move right (C). But wait, we need to think about where the stickman starts.\\n\\nLet me assume the stickman starts in the center or at some position. \\n\\nLooking at the goal: the stickman is on the right (~x=15), ghost on the left (~x=4). The red box is in the middle.\\n\\nIf the stickman starts at center, we might need:\\n1. B (move left to x=4)\\n2. A (jump up)\\n3. C (move right to x=15, clearing the red box while airborne)\\n\\nThis gives BAC, but I need to verify if jumping + moving right would clear the red box.\\n\\nActually, looking more carefully at the positions: Action C moves from left to right. The goal shows exactly this configuration (ghost on left, stickman on right). So C must be the last action.\\n\\nTo avoid the red box in the middle while going from left to right, the stickman needs to be elevated. So action A (jump) before C makes sense.\\n\\nBut first the stickman needs to be on the left side. If the stickman starts in the middle or some default position, B (move left) would get it to the left.\\n\\nSo the sequence is: B (move to left), A (jump up), C (move right while airborne, clearing the red box).\\n\\nWait - does the stickman start at a default position? Looking at the actions again, there's no explicit starting position given. Let me assume a reasonable starting position.\\n\\nIf the stickman starts in the center/right area, then B moves it left, A jumps, C moves right over the red box. Sequence: BAC.\\n\\nActually, I think the simplest interpretation: the stickman needs to end up on the right side, having jumped over the red box. The sequence AC (jump then move right) might work if starting from the left. But if starting from center, we need B first.\\n\\nLet me reconsider - maybe the stickman starts at a default center position. B moves left, then A jumps up, then C moves right over the obstacle. BAC seems right.\\n\\nBut could it be shorter? Just AC? If the stickman starts on the left already... but we don't know the starting position.\\n\\nLooking at the goal image ghost position and the action images, I think the simplest sequence that achieves the goal while avoiding red is: C with a jump. So AC - jump first, then move right while in the air.\\n\\nBut we need to be on the left first. If default position is center, we need B to go left first.\\n\\nI'll go with BAC as the shortest sequence, assuming we start from a default/center position. But if we start from the right side (as might be default), we need BBC... no.\\n\\nActually, let me reconsider the starting position. Many puzzles start with the stickman at a neutral/center position. \\n\\nGiven the goal shows the ghost on the left and stickman on the right, and there's a red box between them, the sequence to avoid the red box would be:\\n1. Get to left position (B - move left)\\n2. Jump (A)  \\n3. Move right over the red box (C)\\n\\nSequence: BAC\",\"sequence\":\"BAC\"}"

The gray figure on the right shows the initial position, and the black figure on the left shows the final position. The dotted arrow points left.\n\nAction C: The stickman moves to the right. The gray figure on the left shows the initial position, and the black figure on the right shows the final position.\n\nNow, let me figure out the starting position. The stickman likely starts at some default position. Let me think about what the goal state requires.\n\nGoal state: The stickman is on the right side of the image (around x=15-16), standing on the ground, with a red obstacle in the middle (around x=9-13). The gray ghost shows a previous position on the left side (around x=4-5).\n\nThe goal shows the stickman on the right side, and there's a red block in the middle that must be avoided. The gray figure on the left suggests the stickman was previously on the left.\n\nIf the stickman starts in a default/center position, we need to get it to the right side while avoiding the red block in the middle.\n\nLooking at action C: it moves the stickman to the right. The goal position matches where action C would place the stickman (right side, around x=15).\n\nBut there's a red block in the middle. If the stickman starts on the left or center and needs to get to the right, it would need to jump over the red block.\n\nWait, let me reconsider. The gray figure in the goal image is on the left side. This likely represents where the stickman was before the last action. \n\nLooking at this more carefully:\n- The goal shows the stickman at the right side (~x=15) standing on the ground\n- There's a red block in the middle that needs to be avoided\n- The gray ghost is on the left (~x=4-5)\n\nIf the stickman starts at a center position, we might need to first move left (B), then jump (A) and move right (C) to get over the red block.\n\nActually, let me reconsider the starting position. Typically in these puzzles, the stickman starts at a central or specific position.\n\nLooking at Action A more carefully - the stickman jumps up from ground level. The initial position seems to be at the center (~x=9).\n\nIf we start at center (x~9-10):\n- The red block is at x~9-13, so the stickman is right at or near the red block\n- We need to get to x~15 on the right\n\nOption 1: Just do C (move right) - but this might go through the red block\nOption 2: A then C - jump up, then move right while in the air to clear the red block\n\nWait, but the goal shows the stickman on the ground on the right side, not in the air. So we need to end up on the ground.\n\nActually, looking at the goal image more carefully, the stickman's final position is on the right at ground level. The ghost on the left suggests the previous position.\n\nIf the stickman starts in the center, to avoid the red block and get to the right:\n- We could move left first (B) to get away from the red block area\n- Then jump (A) to get elevation\n- Then move right (C) to land on the other side of the red block\n\nBut actually, with normal physics, jumping (A) gives vertical displacement, and C gives horizontal displacement to the right. If we do A (jump up) and then C (move right while elevated), we could clear the red block.\n\nLet me think about this differently. The sequence AC would mean: jump up, then move right. If the stickman jumps and then moves right while in the air (or the jump gives enough clearance), it could clear the red block and land on the right side.\n\nBut actually, the goal shows the ghost on the LEFT side, meaning before the final action, the stickman was on the left. So perhaps:\n1. First move left with B (to position ~x=4-5)\n2. Then... but we need to get to the right side past the red block\n\nHmm, looking again: the ghost position in the goal matches where action C starts (left side with ghost). So the last action was C (move right).\n\nIf the last action was C, the stickman moved from the left to the right. Before that, the stickman was on the left at ~x=4-5.\n\nStarting from center (~x=9-10), doing B moves left to ~x=4-5. Then doing C moves right to ~x=15. But going from x=4-5 to x=15 via C would pass through the red block area unless the stickman jumps.\n\nSo the sequence might be: B, A, C - move left, jump, move right (clearing the red block while airborne).\n\nOr maybe just: C alone if starting position allows. But the red block is in the way.\n\nActually, re-examining: C moves from left to right. The distance in C is substantial. If we start at center, B takes us left, then C takes us right past the red block. The question is whether C alone from the left side would go over or through the red block.\n\nSince we need to avoid red and normal physics apply, we likely need to jump over it. So: B (move left), A (jump up), C (move right over the block).\n\nThe sequence would be BAC, but let me check if there's anything shorter.\n\nCould we just do AC? Start at center, jump (A), then move right (C) while in the air? That could work if the jump gives enough height to clear the block. But the ghost in the goal is on the left, not the center, suggesting the stickman moved from the left.\n\nActually, I think the simplest interpretation is C alone gets the stickman to the right position shown in the goal. The red block might just be an obstacle that's between positions but C inherently involves the stickman walking around or the movement doesn't cross the block.\n\nBut the instruction says to avoid red, so we need to actively avoid it. The shortest sequence that gets the stickman to the right side while jumping over the red block would be AC (jump up from center, then move right while elevated to clear the block).\n\nHmm, but the ghost position in the goal is on the left (~x=4-5), not center. This suggests the stickman's position before the final move was on the left.\n\nI think the answer is C - just one action to move right. The stickman might start on the left already, and C moves it to the right. The red block is on the ground between positions, but C might inherently clear it since C is a walking action.\n\nWait, but we need to AVOID red. If the path goes through the red block, we can't just walk through it. We'd need to jump over it.\n\nLet me reconsider: maybe the sequence is AC. Start from some position on the left, jump (A) to get elevation, then C to move right and clear the red block while airborne, landing on the right side.\n\nActually, I think the answer is just C. The stickman starts on the left, and action C moves it to the right. The red block is on the ground, but perhaps C's movement path doesn't intersect with the block (the stickman walks around it or it's slightly offset).\n\nNo wait - looking more carefully at positions, the red block IS between the left and right positions. So we definitely need to jump over it.\n\nThe shortest sequence would be AC: Jump (A) to get airborne, then move right (C) to clear the red block.\n\nBut wait - does the stickman start on the left? If it starts in the center, we'd need B first to go left, then A to jump, then C to go right = BAC.\n\nLooking at action images again: in each action, the starting position seems to be wherever the stickman currently is. So:\n- Default start: let's say center or some standard position\n- The goal has the stickman on the right\n\nIf starting from center: C alone moves right but hits red block. AC = jump then move right.\n\nI'll go with AC as the shortest sequence that avoids the red block.",

AC = jump then move right.\\n\\nI'll go with AC as the shortest sequence that avoids the red block.\",\"sequence\":\"AC\"}"

{
  "reasoning": "Let me analyze each action:\n\nAction A: The stickman jumps up. The gray figure shows the initial position (standing on ground) and the black figure shows the final position (higher up). The dotted arrow on the left indicates upward movement. The stickman moves upward (jumps).\n\nAction B: The black stickman is on the left side, and the gray (initial position) is on the right side. This means the stickman moves from right to left.\n\nAction C: The gray stickman (initial) is on the left, and the black stickman (final) is on the right. This means the stickman moves from left to right.\n\nNow let me figure out the starting position. I need to determine where the stickman starts. Looking at the actions, they show relative movements. Let me assume the stickman starts at some default position.\n\nLooking at the GOAL STATE: The stickman (black) is on the right side of the image (around x=15), standing on the ground. There's a gray figure on the left (around x=5) which appears to be a ghost/shadow. There's a red block in the middle (around x=9-13) on the ground that must be avoided.\n\nThe gray figure in the goal likely represents the initial/starting position, and the black figure represents where we need to end up.\n\nSo the stickman starts on the left side and needs to get to the right side, but there's a red block in the middle on the ground that must be avoided.\n\nAction C moves the stickman from left to right. But if we just do C, the stickman would walk through the red block on the ground.\n\nTo avoid the red block, the stickman needs to jump over it. So the sequence would be to jump (A) and move right (C), or some combination.\n\nLooking more carefully:\n- Starting position: left side, on the ground\n- Goal: right side, on the ground\n- Obstacle: red block in the middle on the ground\n\nThe stickman needs to move right while jumping over the red block. \n\nLooking at action C: it moves the stickman from left to right. The distance seems to be about 10 cm based on the ruler.\nLooking at action A: it makes the stickman jump up.\n\nIf we do A (jump up) then C (move right while in the air or after), we might clear the obstacle. But actually, these actions might be sequential - jump up, then move right while airborne or land first.\n\nWait, let me reconsider. With \"normal laws of physics,\" if we jump (A) and then move right (C), the jump would happen first (go up and come back down), then move right - which wouldn't help avoid the block.\n\nPerhaps we need to combine: jump and move simultaneously? But these seem to be sequential actions.\n\nActually, looking at the goal more carefully - the stickman is on the RIGHT side standing on the ground. The gray shadow is on the LEFT. The red block is between them on the ground.\n\nIf actions are sequential and we need to avoid the red block:\n- Action C moves from current position to the right\n- Action A jumps up\n\nMaybe: A (jump up to get above the red block height), then C (move right while elevated)? But with normal physics, after jumping you'd come back down...\n\nHmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the actions can be combined such that if you jump (A) and are in the air, then moving right (C) happens while airborne, clearing the obstacle.\n\nThe simplest interpretation: AC - jump up, then move right (passing over the red block while in the air).\n\nActually, looking at the positions more carefully in the goal image:\n- Gray figure is at roughly x=4-5, standing on ground\n- Black figure is at roughly x=15-16, standing on ground\n- Red block is at roughly x=9-13 on the ground\n\nIn action C, the stickman moves from about x=5 to x=15 (roughly 10cm to the right). That single move would get us from the left position to the right position, but we'd hit the red block.\n\nSo: A (jump) + C (move right) = AC. Jump first to get airborne, then move right to clear the red block. That's 2 actions.\n\nThe sequence AC should work - jump up to avoid the red block, then move right to reach the goal position on the right side.",
  "sequence": "AC"
}
"{\"reasoning\":\"Let me analyze each action:\\n\\nAction A: The stickman jumps up. The gray figure shows the initial position (standing on ground) and the black figure shows the final position (higher up). The dotted arrow on the left indicates upward movement. The stickman moves upward (jumps).\\n\\nAction B: The black stickman is on the left side, and the gray (initial position) is on the right side. This means the stickman moves from right to left.\\n\\nAction C: The gray stickman (initial) is on the left, and the black stickman (final) is on the right. This means the stickman moves from left to right.\\n\\nNow let me figure out the starting position. I need to determine where the stickman starts. Looking at the actions, they show relative movements. Let me assume the stickman starts at some default position.\\n\\nLooking at the GOAL STATE: The stickman (black) is on the right side of the image (around x=15), standing on the ground. There's a gray figure on the left (around x=5) which appears to be a ghost/shadow. There's a red block in the middle (around x=9-13) on the ground that must be avoided.\\n\\nThe gray figure in the goal likely represents the initial/starting position, and the black figure represents where we need to end up.\\n\\nSo the stickman starts on the left side and needs to get to the right side, but there's a red block in the middle on the ground that must be avoided.\\n\\nAction C moves the stickman from left to right. But if we just do C, the stickman would walk through the red block on the ground.\\n\\nTo avoid the red block, the stickman needs to jump over it. So the sequence would be to jump (A) and move right (C), or some combination.\\n\\nLooking more carefully:\\n- Starting position: left side, on the ground\\n- Goal: right side, on the ground\\n- Obstacle: red block in the middle on the ground\\n\\nThe stickman needs to move right while jumping over the red block. \\n\\nLooking at action C: it moves the stickman from left to right. The distance seems to be about 10 cm based on the ruler.\\nLooking at action A: it makes the stickman jump up.\\n\\nIf we do A (jump up) then C (move right while in the air or after), we might clear the obstacle. But actually, these actions might be sequential - jump up, then move right while airborne or land first.\\n\\nWait, let me reconsider. With \\\"normal laws of physics,\\\" if we jump (A) and then move right (C), the jump would happen first (go up and come back down), then move right - which wouldn't help avoid the block.\\n\\nPerhaps we need to combine: jump and move simultaneously? But these seem to be sequential actions.\\n\\nActually, looking at the goal more carefully - the stickman is on the RIGHT side standing on the ground. The gray shadow is on the LEFT. The red block is between them on the ground.\\n\\nIf actions are sequential and we need to avoid the red block:\\n- Action C moves from current position to the right\\n- Action A jumps up\\n\\nMaybe: A (jump up to get above the red block height), then C (move right while elevated)? But with normal physics, after jumping you'd come back down...\\n\\nHmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the actions can be combined such that if you jump (A) and are in the air, then moving right (C) happens while airborne, clearing the obstacle.\\n\\nThe simplest interpretation: AC - jump up, then move right (passing over the red block while in the air).\\n\\nActually, looking at the positions more carefully in the goal image:\\n- Gray figure is at roughly x=4-5, standing on ground\\n- Black figure is at roughly x=15-16, standing on ground\\n- Red block is at roughly x=9-13 on the ground\\n\\nIn action C, the stickman moves from about x=5 to x=15 (roughly 10cm to the right). That single move would get us from the left position to the right position, but we'd hit the red block.\\n\\nSo: A (jump) + C (move right) = AC. Jump first to get airborne, then move right to clear the red block. That's 2 actions.\\n\\nThe sequence AC should work - jump up to avoid the red block, then move right to reach the goal position on the right side.\",\"sequence\":\"AC\"}"

You can perform various actions to move a stickman. You are given 3 labeled actions (A, B, C) each illustrated by an image. Then, given a GOAL image, determine the shortest sequence of actions (Eg. Examine the input images and reason what each of them means and finally come up with the shortest sequence. Your output must be a JSON object with two fields: 'reasoning' and 'sequence'. The 'sequence' field must be a string containing only the capital letters representing the sequence of actions.

Examine the input images and reason what each of them means and finally come up with the shortest sequence. Your output must be a JSON object with two fields: 'reasoning' and 'sequence'. 
                    The 'sequence' field must be a string containing only the capital letters representing the sequence of actions.

{
  "reasoning": "Let me analyze each action:\n\nAction A: The stickman jumps up. The gray figure shows the initial position (standing on the ground) and the black figure shows the final position (higher up). The dotted arrow on the left confirms upward movement.\n\nAction B: The stickman moves to the left. The gray figure (initial) is on the right side, and the black figure (final) is on the left side.\n\nAction C: The stickman moves to the right. The gray figure (initial) is on the left side, and the black figure (final) is on the right side.\n\nNow let me figure out the starting position. Looking at the action images, it seems the stickman starts at some default position. Let me think about this differently.\n\nLooking at Action B: the stickman moves from right (~14cm) to left (~4cm). So B moves left by about 10cm.\nLooking at Action C: the stickman moves from left (~4cm) to right (~14cm). So C moves right by about 10cm.\nLooking at Action A: the stickman jumps up from the ground.\n\nNow the GOAL STATE: The black stickman is on the right side (~15cm), and there's a gray ghost/shadow on the left (~4cm). There's a red block in the middle (around 8-13cm on the ground). The stickman needs to avoid the red block.\n\nThe goal shows the stickman on the right side with its previous position shown in gray on the left. This looks like the result of action C (moving from left to right). But there's a red block in the middle that needs to be avoided.\n\nWait, let me reconsider. The gray figure likely represents where the stickman was previously. So the goal is to have the stickman end up on the right side (~15cm) having come from the left side (~4cm), while avoiding the red block in the middle.\n\nIf the stickman starts at a default position (let's say center or left), it needs to get to the right side. But there's a red block in the middle, so it can't just walk/move right through it - it needs to jump over it.\n\nStarting position: Let's assume the stickman starts at the center or some default position.\n\nActually, looking more carefully at the images:\n- The goal shows the stickman on the right with gray shadow on the left\n- The red block is between them on the ground\n\nTo avoid the red block while moving from left to right, the stickman would need to jump (A) while moving right (C), or jump first then move.\n\nIf actions are sequential: the stickman needs to be on the left first, then jump up (A) to get airborne, then move right (C) to clear the red block. Or perhaps action A combined with movement.\n\nBut wait - looking at A again, it only moves up (no horizontal movement). B moves left, C moves right.\n\nIf the stickman starts at a default center position, let's think about what needs to happen:\n1. First move left (B) to get to the left position\n2. Then jump (A) to get above the red block\n3. Then move right (C) to land on the right side\n\nBut actually, if the stickman is already in the air from action A, then action C might move it right while it's still in the air (or it might land first and then move).\n\nHmm, if normal physics apply, jumping up (A) would bring you back down to the same spot. Moving right (C) would be a ground-level movement.\n\nThe simplest interpretation: the stickman needs to go from left to right, jumping over the red block. The sequence would be: first get to the left (B if needed), then jump and move right.\n\nActually, if we assume the stickman starts in the middle/default position:\n- B moves it to the left (~4cm position)\n- A makes it jump up\n- C moves it to the right (~14cm position) - if done while in air, it clears the red block\n\nSo the sequence could be: BAC\n\nBut let me reconsider if the stickman starts at the left already. Looking at action images, the stickman seems to start at a centered position.\n\nWait - looking at the goal more carefully, the positions match action C exactly (gray on left at ~4cm, black on right at ~15cm), except there's a red block in the middle. So the goal is essentially action C but with an obstacle.\n\nIf the stickman starts at the center, we need B to go left first, then jump A, then C to go right. That's BAC.\n\nBut if it starts at the left already, just AC would work (jump then move right while airborne).\n\nActually, considering normal physics - if you jump (A) straight up, you come back down. Then if you do C (move right), you'd walk into the red block. So you need to be in the air while moving right.\n\nPerhaps the sequence AC means: jump up, then while in the air move right, clearing the obstacle. That would be the shortest at 2 actions.\n\nBut the stickman's starting position matters. If it starts at center, we'd need B first to move left, then A to jump, then C to move right: BAC.\n\nLooking at the goal image again, the stickman ends up at right (~15cm) with ghost at left (~4cm). The red block is roughly at 8-13cm. This matches going from left to right.\n\nIf default starting position is center, B moves to left, then AC to jump over the red to the right = BAC (3 steps).\n\nI think the answer is BAC.",
  "sequence": "BAC"
}

Let me think about the starting position. \n\nLooking at actions B and C, the stickman appears to be at different horizontal positions. In B, the stickman starts on the right and moves to the left. In C, the stickman starts on the left and moves to the right.\n\nThe goal shows the stickman on the right side (around x=15) with a red block in the middle (around x=9-13). The gray \"ghost\" position is on the left (around x=4-5).\n\nIf the stickman starts at a default/center position, I need to figure out the initial state. Looking at action A, the stickman seems to be centered around x=8-9.\n\nLet me reconsider. The starting position seems to be where the stickman is standing initially. There's a red block in the middle area that needs to be avoided.\n\nIf the stickman starts in the center/default position, to get to the right side, I could use action C (move right). But there's a red block in the middle. \n\nWait - looking more carefully at the goal image: the stickman is already on the right, and the gray ghost is on the left. The red block is between them on the ground. The stickman needs to have gotten past the red block.\n\nIf the stickman starts at a left/center position and needs to get to the right while avoiding the red block on the ground, it would need to jump over it. So the sequence might be: jump up (A) and then move right (C), or move right while jumping.\n\nActually, let me reconsider the positions more carefully:\n\n- In the goal, the black stickman is at approximately x=15, standing on the ground\n- The gray ghost (previous position) is at approximately x=4-5\n- The red block is at approximately x=9-13 on the ground\n\nIf the stickman starts in the center, it needs to move right past the red block. Since the red block is on the ground, the stickman needs to jump (A) to go over it, and also move right (C).\n\nThe sequence would need to combine jumping and moving right. If we do A (jump up) then C (move right), the stickman would jump up first, then while still in the air (or after landing) move right. But if it lands back in the same spot after jumping, then moving right would put it on or past the red block.\n\nActually, looking at the positions more carefully:\n- Action C moves the stickman from about x=4 to x=15 (a large rightward movement)\n- The starting position seems to be around x=4-5 based on the gray ghost in the goal\n\nIf the stickman starts at x=4-5 (left side), doing C would move it to x=15 (right side), but the red block is in between on the ground, so it would collide.\n\nThe stickman needs to jump (A) to get above the red block, then move right (C) while airborne to clear it. Or jump first, then move right.\n\nBut wait - looking at action A again, it moves the stickman upward significantly. If I do A first (jump up high), then C (move right), the stickman would be high enough to clear the red block while moving right, then land on the right side.\n\nSo the sequence would be AC - jump up, then move right (clearing the red block while still elevated).\n\nActually, I need to think about whether A keeps the stickman in the air or if it lands. With \"normal laws of physics,\" the stickman would go up and come back down. So A alone would result in the stickman back on the ground.\n\nHmm, but if we do A (jump) and then immediately C (move right while in the air), the stickman could clear the red block. If I do A first (jump up high), then C (move right), the stickman would be high enough to clear the red block while moving right, then land on the right side.\\n\\nSo the sequence would be AC - jump up, then move right (clearing the red block while still elevated).\\n\\nActually, I need to think about whether A keeps the stickman in the air or if it lands. With \\\"normal laws of physics,\\\" the stickman would go up and come back down. So A alone would result in the stickman back on the ground.\\n\\nHmm, but if we do A (jump) and then immediately C (move right while in the air), the stickman could clear the red block. The sequence AC should work.\\n\\nThe shortest sequence is AC.\",\"sequence\":\"AC\"}"

This suggests the stickman started on the left and needs to end on the right.\n\nIf the stickman starts on the left and needs to get to the right, with a red block in the middle, it needs to jump over the red block. \n\nThe sequence AC could mean: jump up (A), then while elevated, move right (C).
